ontology.foodex2_description: Cakes, pastries and similar baked products
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code exists specifically for tiramisu cake. A00LC (Cakes, pastries and similar baked products) is the closest defensible parent category.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard slice of tiramisu cake weighing approximately 120 g, as typically served in a café or restaurant setting in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Tiramisu, USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB No. 19402)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for tiramisu (NDB 19402 / FDC ID 169655) used as the primary nutrient reference. Values represent a standard tiramisu preparation with ladyfingers, mascarpone, eggs, sugar, espresso, and cocoa. The Singapore restaurant product 'Italian Bliss Tiramisu Cake' from Kazo Premium is a commercial cake-format variant; nutrient values are adapted from the USDA tiramisu reference as no HPB Singapore or direct branded analysis is available. Minor adjustments to sugar and fat are within expected commercial variation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for tiramisu cake specifically. GI of 62 is estimated based on the closest analogous dessert category from the University of Sydney GI Database: sponge cake and cream-based desserts with high sugar content typically fall in the medium GI range (55–70). The ladyfinger biscuit base (GI ~46–52 for plain sponge biscuits) combined with high-sugar mascarpone cream and espresso yields an estimated composite GI of approximately 62 (medium). Glycemic load per 120 g serving = (62 × 29.5 × 1.2) / 100 = approximately 22.0 g. This is an analogous estimate; direct measurement is recommended for clinical use.

hypertension.guidance: Tiramisu cake contains approximately 95 mg sodium and 9.8 g saturated fat per 100 g; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to one small slice and monitor overall daily sodium and saturated fat intake, as regular consumption of high-fat desserts may contribute to elevated blood pressure over time.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High sugar content and medium glycemic index
type2_diabetes.guidance: Tiramisu cake contains approximately 20 g of sugar per 100 g with a medium glycemic index of 62, resulting in a glycemic load of approximately 22 per standard serving; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should treat this as an occasional indulgence, limit portion to half a standard slice, and pair with a protein-rich food to blunt the postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat, cholesterol, and trans fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Tiramisu cake provides 17.5 g total fat, 9.8 g saturated fat, and approximately 115 mg cholesterol per 100 g, along with trace trans fat; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption to occasional small portions as part of a diet otherwise low in saturated fat and cholesterol, consistent with AHA dietary guidelines.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tiramisu cake is an Italian-origin chilled dessert widely served in Singapore cafés and restaurants. It is constructed by layering espresso-soaked ladyfinger biscuits (savoiardi) with a rich, aerated cream made from mascarpone cheese, eggs, and sugar, then finishing with a dusting of unsweetened cocoa powder. The cake-format variant, such as the 'Italian Bliss Tiramisu Cake' served at Kazo Premium in Singapore, is typically assembled in a round or rectangular cake mould and sliced for individual service. The texture is soft, creamy, and slightly spongy, with a bittersweet coffee flavour balanced by the sweetness of the mascarpone cream. Tiramisu is not baked; it is assembled cold and refrigerated until set. Nutritionally, a standard 120 g slice provides approximately 353 kcal, 21 g fat (of which 11.8 g is saturated), 35.4 g carbohydrate, and 24 g sugar. The medium glycemic index (estimated 62) and high sugar content make it a high-concern food for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or cardiovascular disease. It contains gluten (ladyfingers), dairy (mascarpone, cream), and eggs, making it unsuitable for those with these allergens. Tiramisu is not halal-certified in most standard preparations due to the use of alcohol-soaked ladyfingers in traditional recipes, though alcohol-free versions exist. In Singapore, tiramisu cake is popular as a café dessert, celebration cake, and premium takeaway item.

confidence_reason: No direct HPB Singapore or branded nutritional analysis is available for this specific product. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy tiramisu entry (FDC ID 169655), which represents a standard tiramisu preparation. The GI is an analogous estimate based on comparable desserts in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium because the source match is adapted rather than exact.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 169655, tiramisu) as no HPB Singapore or direct branded analysis exists for this product. Values are representative of a standard tiramisu preparation. 2. GI of 62 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for tiramisu cake was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. Direct measurement is recommended for clinical use. 3. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy tiramisu entry. 4. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy tiramisu entry. 5. Vitamin C is 0.0; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central — tiramisu contains no vitamin C-contributing ingredients. 6. The food_name has been normalised to 'Tiramisu Cake' as the canonical English name; the original GrabFood menu name 'Italian Bliss Tiramisu Cake' is preserved in aliases. 7. Halal status is marked false as traditional tiramisu may contain alcohol-soaked ladyfingers; the specific Kazo Premium product's halal certification status is unknown. 8. Trans fat of 0.2 g per 100 g reflects trace amounts from dairy fat (naturally occurring); this is not an industrial trans fat addition.
